title: The Book of Khalid
description: The Book of Khalid — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "The Book of Khalid" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Abbas Effendi
memes.description: The leader of the Bahá'í (Babist) faith based in Haifa, whose spiritual authority draws Mrs. Gotfry on annual pilgrimage and serves as a counterpoint to both institutional Islam and Western materialism in The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: Abdul Hamid
memes.description: Sultan Abdul Hamid II of the Ottoman Empire (r. 1876–1909), referenced in The Book of Khalid as the embodiment of autocratic tyranny, censorship, and the decaying Ottoman regime.

memes.label: Ahmed Bey
memes.description: A Syrian friend of Khalid's in Beirut who introduces him to Mrs. Gotfry and serves as a cultural intermediary — the worldly Easterner at home in Western society.

memes.label: "Al-Fatihah"
memes.description: "The Opening Word — Khalid introduces his Book."

memes.label: "Al-Khatimah"
memes.description: "The Closing Word — Khalid concludes his Book."

memes.label: Al-Mutanabbi
memes.description: Classical Arabic poet (915–965) whose divan Shakib carries throughout the narrative as literary companion and cultural anchor.

memes.label: Baalbek
memes.description: Khalid's birthplace and childhood home in Lebanon — the village at the foot of the ancient Roman ruins, representing the traditional Eastern world he must leave to find himself.

memes.label: Bahaullah
memes.description: Founder of the Baha'i Faith (1817–1892), known in the novel as "the Baha," whose teachings Mrs. Gotfry champions and Khalid selectively engages with.

memes.label: Battery Park
memes.description: Public park at the southern tip of Manhattan where Khalid and Shakib spend their Sunday afternoons loafing, philosophizing, and observing the human spectacle of New York.

memes.label: Beirut
memes.description: Port city and gateway of emigration from Ottoman Syria, where Khalid and Shakib depart Lebanon for their journey to America.

memes.label: Bohemianism
memes.description: The artistic, unconventional lifestyle that Khalid embraces in 1890s New York — a sphere of freedom, creativity, and danger where "the poison and the antidote are frequently offered together."

memes.label: Book as Propylon
memes.description: The book's self-conception as a "propylon" (gateway) through which races, cultures, and epochs are brought together — a literary work that functions not as an end in itself but as a portal between civilizations.

memes.label: Boss O'Donohue
memes.description: The Tammany Hall political boss who recruits Khalid as a canvasser, ridicules his philosophy, and has him imprisoned — the embodiment of machine politics crushing idealistic reform.

memes.label: Brooklyn Bridge
memes.description: New York City landmark spanning the East River — immortalised in Khalid's comic poem and among the first wonders he beholds upon arriving in America.

memes.label: Cairo
memes.description: Egyptian capital and intellectual destination where the Khedivial Library houses the manuscript that frames the novel, and where Khalid seeks medical care in his final days.

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Production notes and colophon."

memes.label: Committee of Union and Progress
memes.description: The Ottoman reform movement (Young Turks) that appears in Khalid's story as both a threat and a symbol of the political currents reshaping the late Ottoman world.

memes.label: Constantinople
memes.description: Ottoman imperial capital and seat of the Sultan — the political-judicial destination Khalid never reaches, where he imagines a Socratic trial that Mrs. Gotfry prevents.

memes.label: Damascus
memes.description: The ancient Syrian city where Khalid delivers his prophetic message of reform — the stronghold of Islamic orthodoxy that he challenges with his call for "the emigration of the mind."

memes.label: Democracy and Its Dowry
memes.description: Khalid's critical concept of American democracy — the gap between democratic ideals and the reality of machine politics, corruption, and imprisonment that he experienced firsthand in New York.

memes.label: Dervish Philosophy
memes.description: Khalid's adopted persona and philosophy — the wandering dervish who renounces worldly ambition to pursue truth through direct experience, embodying the synthesis of Eastern mysticism and Western rationalism.

memes.label: Diogenes
memes.description: The Greek Cynic philosopher (c. 412–323 BCE) invoked in The Book of Khalid as a symbol of radical simplicity and contempt for fame — the ancient model for Sheikh Taleb's asceticism.

memes.label: "*Diwan al-Mutanabbi*"
memes.description: The collected poems of the tenth-century Arab poet al-Mutanabbi, Shakib's constant literary companion throughout The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: East–West Duality
memes.description: The central philosophical tension in The Book of Khalid between Eastern spirituality and Western materialism. Khalid's gospel seeks to fuse the "strenuosity of Europe" with the "ease of the Orient," synthesizing ambition with contentment into a higher human consciousness.

memes.label: Ellis Island
memes.description: New York immigration station — the "third and last station" of the emigrant's Via Dolorosa, where Khalid faces detention and potential deportation.

memes.label: "Endnotes"
memes.description: "Endnotes and references for the text."

memes.label: Exile and Belonging
memes.description: The existential condition of the immigrant caught between two civilizations — neither fully Eastern nor Western — and Khalid's attempt to forge identity from this duality rather than resolve it.

memes.label: Father Farouche
memes.description: A Jesuit priest in Zahleh who visits Khalid's family home, acts as a spy for the Ottoman government, and schemes to marry Najma to a Medjidi Bey while Khalid is imprisoned.

memes.label: Hashish Vision
memes.description: The hashish-induced visionary experience in Book I, Chapter I — a moment of expanded consciousness that reveals Khalid's philosophical ambitions and establishes the theme of altered perception as a path to truth.

memes.label: Henry David Thoreau
memes.description: American naturalist and philosopher (1817–1862) invoked in the novel as a model of solitude practiced rightly — filtered through Nature, not cocooned from the world.

memes.label: "*Histoire Intime*"
memes.description: Shakib's fictional intimate biography of Khalid, the embedded manuscript that serves as a key source within the narrative frame of The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: Im-Hanna
memes.description: A Syrian peddleress in New York who shares living quarters with Khalid and Shakib — a figure of maternal warmth, practical wisdom, and domestic stability in the immigrant community.

memes.label: Immanent Morality
memes.description: Khalid's theory that morality is innate and immanent in human intelligence, transmitted across generations through trust and faith in human nature.

memes.label: "Imprint"
memes.description: "Publishing information and copyright notice."

memes.label: East-West Synthesis — The Central Dialectic of The Book of Khalid
memes.description: A systematic analysis of the East-West dialectic that structures every layer of The Book of Khalid — from geography and character to philosophy and prose style — and Rihani's argument for synthesis over opposition.

memes.label: Khalid's Journey — A Geographic and Spiritual Trajectory
memes.description: A synthetic analysis of Khalid's geographic movements across three continents as a map of his spiritual development — from Lebanese village to New York underworld to prophetic return.

memes.label: The Philosophical Dialogue Network in The Book of Khalid
memes.description: A synthetic map of how Western philosophers — Carlyle, Rousseau, Emerson, Nietzsche, Thoreau, Whitman, Spencer — function as an intertextual dialogue network shaping Khalid's intellectual development.

memes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
memes.description: Genevan philosopher (1712–1778) whose *Confessions* and *Emile* appear in the narrative as key texts in Khalid's intellectual and moral formation.

memes.label: Khalid
memes.description: The protagonist and title character — a Lebanese immigrant in America who develops a philosophy of immanent morality and becomes a prophetic reformer upon returning to the East.

memes.label: Khalid's Banishment
memes.description: Khalid's expulsion from his father's house in Baalbek after refusing to attend church, orchestrated by Jesuit influence over his family.

memes.label: Khalid's Departure from Lebanon
memes.description: The event in which Khalid and Shakib leave their homeland, smuggled from the port of Beirut, setting sail for America. This departure is the novel's pivotal turning point — the moment that transforms a provincial youth into a transatlantic figure, and the event that inaugurates the East–West dialectic at the heart of Rihani's philosophical project.

memes.label: Khalid's Imprisonment
memes.description: Khalid's ten-day imprisonment in the New York Tombs after offending Boss O'Donohue — the crucible in which he "broods on Democracy and the Dowry of Democracy" and writes the prison chapter of the K.L. MS.

memes.label: Khedivial Library
memes.description: The national library of Egypt in Cairo where the manuscript of Khalid's book is discovered among the papyri, providing the narrative frame for the entire novel.

memes.label: Love Sacred and Profane
memes.description: The duality of love in Khalid's experience — the tension between spiritual devotion and carnal desire, between the "houri" and the sacred, which mirrors the book's larger East-West dialectic.

memes.label: Maronite Church
memes.description: The Eastern Catholic church in communion with Rome that governs the spiritual and communal life of Khalid's Lebanese homeland in The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: Marseilles
memes.description: French port city where Khalid and Shakib are swindled by sharpers during their emigrant journey to America — a station of suffering on the emigrant's Via Dolorosa.

memes.label: Monsieur Pascal
memes.description: The 17th-century French philosopher and mathematician whose *Thoughts* (Pensées) profoundly influenced the young Khalid, sparking his turn toward the Bible and the Prophets.

memes.label: Mount Lebanon
memes.description: The mountain range that dominates the landscape of Khalid's homeland. In The Book of Khalid, Mount Lebanon is both a physical place of origin — Baalbek lies "a little way from the mountain-road to the Cedars of Lebanon" — and a symbolic threshold between the world Khalid leaves and the world he seeks. It is the last thing he sees when departing and the landscape that anchors his spiritual identity.

memes.label: Mr. Hoolihan
memes.description: Secretary to Boss O'Donohue and Tammany Hall functionary who sends Khalid the fateful letter offering a "Canvassership" — the bureaucratic face of machine politics.

memes.label: Mrs. Gotfry
memes.description: An American woman in Beirut, a votary of Abbas Effendi (Baha'ism), who engages Khalid in theological and philosophical discussion — representing the Western spiritual seeker drawn to Eastern faith.

memes.label: Najma
memes.description: Khalid's cousin and first love — the woman he was "not permitted to marry," whose memory haunts his subsequent relationships and shapes his understanding of love and loss.

memes.label: Nature as Temple
memes.description: The Lebanese landscape — particularly the Cedars and mountains — as a site of spiritual revelation, an alternative temple to the mosque and church, where Khalid finds his deepest connection to the divine.

memes.label: New York City
memes.description: The primary American setting of the book — the city where Khalid works as a lawyer's clerk, enters Bohemia, endures Tammany Hall politics, and develops his philosophy of immanent morality.

memes.label: Oriental Spirituality
memes.description: The Rihani-conceived spiritual essence of the East — continence, purity, magnanimity — contrasted with Western materialism, forming the central East-West dialectic of the book.

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: French capital, invoked by Khalid through Rousseau's epithet "the city of mud" — a cosmopolitan benchmark and symbol of European political traditions critiqued in the novel.

memes.label: Prophetic Reform
memes.description: Khalid's doctrine of nonviolent social transformation — "the emigration of the mind before the revolution of the state" — delivered as a prophetic call to the people of Damascus.

memes.label: Ralph Waldo Emerson
memes.description: American transcendentalist philosopher (1803–1882) invoked in the novel as an authority on Nature and solitude, whose Concord pines Khalid visits as literary pilgrimage.

memes.label: Revolt Against Conformity
memes.description: Khalid's life-defining rebellion against the "dominating spirit of one's people and ancestors" — the money-seeking ethos shared by both Syrian and American societies — and his pursuit of authentic selfhood through principled revolt.

memes.label: Shakib
memes.description: Khalid's devoted friend, scribe, and chronicler — the narrator-figure who preserves and transmits the "K.L. MS." and whose own voice merges with the book's narration.

memes.label: Sheikh Taleb
memes.description: A philosopher-sage of Damascus who lives in self-imposed obscurity in New York, subsisting on oranges and dispensing wisdom to Khalid and Shakib. He embodies the Rihani ideal of direct communion with the divine, unmediated by institutional authority.

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: The Greek philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE) repeatedly invoked in The Book of Khalid as the model of non-institutional wisdom — one who "educates not by lesson, but by going about his business."

memes.label: Soul's Journey
memes.description: The book's framing conceit — "the chart and history of one little kingdom of the Soul" — Khalid's life as an allegory of the soul's progress through experience, error, and awakening.

memes.label: Tammany Hall
memes.description: The New York Democratic Party political machine that recruits and then destroys Khalid — the embodiment of corrupt American democracy against which his idealistic philosophy collides.

memes.label: The Atlantic Voyage
memes.description: Khalid's brutal journey from Beirut to New York — "huddled like sheep on deck... and like cattle transported under hatches" — the physical ordeal that marks his passage from the Old World to the New.

memes.label: The Book of Khalid
memes.description: The book itself as a self-conscious literary artifact — "the chart and history of one little kingdom of the Soul" — a genre-defying work that is simultaneously novel, philosophical treatise, and spiritual autobiography.

memes.label: The Hashish Gathering
memes.description: The early episode in which Khalid, under the influence of hashish, delivers an impromptu philosophical discourse and receives his defining epithets — "the dervish of science" and "the ropedancer of metaphysics."

memes.label: The Jesuits
memes.description: The Catholic religious order that educated Khalid in Beirut — representing Western religious institutional influence in the East and the disciplined intellectual training that shaped his mind.

memes.label: The K.L. Manuscript
memes.description: Khalid's own writings — the "K.L. MS." quoted throughout the book — a philosophical and poetic manuscript that Shakib preserves and the narrator cites as the ultimate source of Khalid's thoughts.

memes.label: Ottoman Empire
memes.description: The declining Islamic empire that ruled the Levant, including Khalid's homeland, during the events of The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: The Pyramids
memes.description: Ancient monuments on the Giza plateau near Cairo, invoked by Rihani as a symbol of immensity and endurance, contrasted with the modern skyscraper and the written word.

memes.label: The Ulema
memes.description: The Islamic religious authorities of Damascus whom Khalid challenges with his reform message — the institutional guardians of orthodoxy representing the stagnation he seeks to overcome.

memes.label: The Wedding of Khalid and Najma
memes.description: Khalid's attempt to marry his cousin Najma in Baalbek, obstructed by the Maronite Church's dispensation system and Jesuit interference.

memes.label: Thomas Carlyle
memes.description: Scottish philosopher and historian (1795–1881) whose works Hero-Worship and Latter-Day Pamphlets serve as catalysts for Khalid's intellectual and political awakening in The Book of Khalid.

memes.label: "To God"
memes.description: "Book III dedication: Khalid addresses his Maker God. In Kulmakan"

memes.label: "To Man"
memes.description: "Book I dedication: Khalid addresses his Brother Man. In the Exchange"

memes.label: "To Nature"
memes.description: "Book II dedication: Khalid addresses his Mother Nature. In the Temple"

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Public domain dedication and license information."

memes.label: Walt Whitman
memes.description: American poet (1819–1892) whose democratic expansiveness and refusal of tears makes him, in Khalid's view, the anti-sentimental model of the self-reliant poet.
